video_title: The Fireworks Show Is Over: Why This Is An AI Rotation, Not a Bubble Unwind
gist: Jordi Visser argues the week's S&P/Nasdaq drop is a healthy rotation out of crowded memory/semis into healthcare, financials, and the application layer (notably Eli Lilly) rather than the start of a bear market, while warning that memory (Micron, SK Hynix) faces multiple compression and that the real winners are the optical/edge and AI-pharma application layers.

insights.claim: Visser reads the week's selloff (S&P -2.6%, Qs -4.5%) as a necessary rotation, not a bear market, noting the S&P just made its highest weekly RSI since 2024 and that sustainable tops require RSI divergences that have not yet appeared.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Argues against fading the market on the dip; the signal to watch is a new high accompanied by RSI divergence before turning defensive.
insights.quote: Looks like a bare market is starting doesn't at all to me.

insights.claim: On Friday the S&P fell ~3% but five sectors were up, with the decliners being AI-linked names and capital rotating into healthcare and financials (the day's two strongest sectors).
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Confirms rotation thesis with breadth; capital exiting chips is staying in the market, favoring healthcare/financials over AI hardware near-term.
insights.quote: The capital that exited the chip complex didn't leave the market. It rotated into healthcare and financials.

insights.claim: A sustained bear market would require macro to break first (credit spreads widening, jobless claims rising, ISM/PMI falling, earnings declining, margins compressing); none of these are present, making the current setup the 'photographic negative' of a bear market.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Provides a concrete checklist for when to flip bearish; until credit spreads (which 'never lie') turn, dips are buyable.
insights.quote: Credit spreads never lie. They always lead.

insights.claim: On Friday the Morgan Stanley volatility/beta long-short factor fell ~10.5%, the largest move since around 2000, and the sector-neutral pure-beta version had its worst day since Liberation Day, indicating a positioning cleanse rather than a fundamental break.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A factor cleanse de-risks the market and supports the rotation-not-collapse read; positioning unwinds, not fundamentals, drove the drop.

insights.claim: Visser is structurally cautious on memory (DRAM/HBM), having exited Micron in the $600-700 area (it closed high $800s), arguing the easy doubles/triples are gone and risk/reward has deteriorated now that everyone is bullish.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Flags memory as a crowded, late-stage trade to avoid even if not outright shorted; he'd re-engage Micron only near $500-400.
insights.quote: I do not think it is the place to have your capital.

insights.claim: Recursive self-improvement (RSI) is the biggest bearish risk for memory because AI could discover memory-efficient architectures that reduce HBM needed for intelligence, and 'when this starts happening, you won't be able to get out fast enough.'
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Identifies a non-obvious, fast-moving tail risk to the memory thesis; algorithmic efficiency gains could collapse demand extrapolations.
insights.quote: Recursive self-improvement could allow AI to solve its own memory bottleneck.

insights.claim: Visser warns against translating token-consumption growth directly into memory demand (as Goldman's DRAM-undersupply-until-2028 call does), because efficiency gains, algorithmic solutions, and high memory prices themselves will create substitutes.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Cautions investors not to extrapolate bullish memory models 5 years out; the same math error bears made on the downside, bulls now make on the upside.
insights.quote: you cannot translate token consumption into memory

insights.claim: Korean retail has gone all-in on SK Hynix, Samsung and the Korean market while breadth there is poor and construction/machinery sectors are breaking down to one-month lows, a divergence Visser sees as a warning.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Crowded retail positioning plus deteriorating breadth in Korea is a setup for memory underperformance; a sentiment red flag.

insights.claim: Rising adoption of cheaper Chinese open-source models like DeepSeek (which topped a US business-spending index) and on-device/edge models is a structural demand risk for centralized frontier models and memory.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Open-source/edge substitution could commoditize frontier inference and undercut memory demand; watch any peak in Anthropic/frontier usage charts.

insights.claim: Visser frames a regime shift from a labor-vs-capital cycle to a compute-vs-energy one, where the AI 'production function' is electrons in, tokens out, and digital workers consume compute/memory/electricity instead of wages, removing humans and credit from the cycle.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A reusable macro model: AI cycles break on physical bottlenecks (power, chips, networking) not on credit/layoffs, changing which indicators matter.
insights.quote: The input is electrons. The output is tokens. That is the new production function.

insights.claim: Because AI agents don't buy houses, autos, or hire/fire, the traditional credit-and-labor business cycle no longer applies, making housing, private credit, private equity, VC and real estate 'stuck' rather than crashing.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Implies traditional recession indicators tied to housing/autos are obsolete; tokenization is pitched as the unlock for these 'stuck' dormant assets.
insights.quote: AI agents do not buy houses. They do not buy autos.

insights.claim: Visser is negative on all Fortune 500 companies by 2030, arguing established firms will struggle to adopt AI while high-margin AI-native businesses scale revenue without scaling labor or debt.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A long-horizon short thesis on incumbents; favors AI-native, high-margin businesses over legacy large-caps over a 5-year view.

themes.theme: Capex-vs-revenue tension and the ROI phase

insights.claim: IBM's leadership (via Norges Bank podcast) estimates one gigawatt of AI data-center power needs roughly $60-80 billion of semiconductors alone, and doubts current revenue pools can support all the capex, signaling the market is shifting from a 'capacity land-grab' to an 'ROI discrimination' phase.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing IBM CEO)
insights.so_what: The next AI phase rewards utilization, pricing power and payback, not just chip-securing; differentiate names by ROI rather than buying the whole complex.

insights.claim: Jensen Huang says cost per gigawatt for an AI factory is rising to $80-100 billion from $50, even as enterprise adoption lags far behind the cost curve, raising the risk of disappointment.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing Jensen Huang)
insights.so_what: Escalating per-gigawatt costs against lagging adoption means some capex won't be needed; supports selectivity and ROI focus over blanket exposure.

insights.claim: A wave of huge AI-related capital raises (Alphabet's record $85 billion, Meta's big equity raise, plus IPOs and debt) is being done to fund the chip buildout, not to monetize for existing investors, which Visser reads as a strong 'enough cash exists' signal but also a top-like behavior.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Front-running capital raises confirm capex demand and funding availability; the $85B raise barely moving the stock signals abundant liquidity for the buildout.
insights.quote: Alphabet's record-breaking $85 billion raise for Google AI business is a hell of a good signal.

insights.claim: JP Morgan analysis of satellite images shows over 60% of data-center capacity planned for 2027 completion has not begun, and WSTS forecasts semiconductor growth decelerating from ~90% year-on-year toward 2027.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser (citing JP Morgan, WSTS)
insights.so_what: Physical buildout delays and a decelerating second derivative argue chip/memory names need repricing for risk even if long-term demand holds.

themes.theme: Where Visser is buying: optical, edge, application layer

insights.claim: Visser strongly prefers Marvell over Micron, citing Jensen Huang calling Marvell a future trillion-dollar company (~$250B now), an early-stage optical/co-packaged-optics (CPO) opportunity with less competition; the stock was up ~30% on the week plus 7% after hours.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Optical/CPO is positioned as the earlier-cycle, less-crowded hardware trade versus crowded memory; Marvell is his preferred name.
insights.quote: Give me that one all day long guys over Micron at this point.

insights.claim: Visser argues Eli Lilly could become the largest US company by the end of the decade (larger than Nvidia) and 'the most important AI company in the world,' running a 'Lilypod' of 1,000+ Blackwell GPUs trained on its own data, with revenue growing ~55% year-over-year.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Frames the AI application/ROI layer (specialized models on proprietary data) as the next leg; Eli Lilly is his marquee application-layer pick (made all-time highs Friday).
insights.quote: Peptides are this decade's API keys.

insights.claim: Visser bought Bitcoin and silver on weakness, viewing Bitcoin as in a bear market until it reclaims the 200-day moving average, and bets on crypto rails as the settlement infrastructure for a coming machine-speed agent economy.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Treats Bitcoin near its 200-week MA as a long-term accumulation zone tied to agentic-economy settlement rails, not a momentum trade.
insights.quote: I believe it is the only highquality stock that will exist for certainty in a decade.

insights.claim: Visser bought Exxon and Chevron as defensive hedges, arguing energy is mispriced given the closed Strait of Hormuz and that oil is likely to migrate higher or hold, providing a portfolio hedge against AI-name volatility.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Energy is positioned as a defensive hedge against rising oil and AI volatility; prediction markets show Strait normalization below 50% probability.

insights.claim: Visser flags batteries (e.g., Fluence, up 44% on the week) as a multi-year necessity for data centers, having predicted before the announcement that Fluence would integrate battery storage with Nvidia/Siemens for flexible data-center power.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Battery storage is pitched as an under-appreciated data-center power play; Fluence's hyperscaler master supply agreements validate the theme.

insights.claim: Visser advocates using multiple LLMs to assess recession risk objectively (paste the same bearish data into all five and average the answers) because LLMs lack the perma-bear bias of human economists.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A practical workflow for de-biasing macro analysis; treat LLM ensembles as a sentiment-neutral check on recession calls.

insights.claim: Visser endorses the idea (via Michael Batnick) that experience can hurt investors during regime changes because 'pattern recognition can become pattern imprisonment'—experts become anchored to an earlier version of the world.
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: A meta-warning that veteran bears may be 'watching the wrong movie'; supports re-underwriting the AI regime rather than applying old cycle templates.
insights.quote: pattern recognition can be become pattern imprisonment

insights.claim: The S&P up nine weeks/nine days in a row on the back of earnings is historically an explosive, not faded, signal, and current forward P/E is not bubble territory (a 20% drop with flat earnings would leave it cheap).
insights.speaker: Jordi Visser
insights.so_what: Strong momentum plus non-bubble valuation argues against fading the index; reinforces buy-the-dip over short-the-market.
insights.quote: This is not a bubble, guys.
